content:
# Exodus Chapter 27 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~49r–49v (PDF pp. 95–96); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 69–70 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_027_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Bronze Altar — Where Sacrifice Meets Heaven (vv. 1–8)

**1** "Build the altar out of acacia wood — five cubits long and five cubits wide, making it perfectly square, and three cubits tall. **2** Make horns on each of its four corners, so that the horns are all of one piece with the altar, and overlay it with bronze. **3** Make its pots for removing ashes, and its shovels, its basins for catching the blood, its meat forks, and its firepans. Make all its utensils out of bronze. **4** Make a grating for it — a network of bronze mesh — and make four bronze rings on the four corners of the mesh. **5** Set the mesh under the ledge of the altar so that it reaches halfway up the altar. **6** Make poles of acacia wood for the altar and overlay them with bronze. **7** The poles are to be inserted into the rings so that they are on both sides of the altar when it is carried. **8** Make the altar hollow, out of boards. Just as you were shown on the mountain, so it must be made."

---

### The Courtyard — Defining Sacred Space (vv. 9–19)

**9** "Make the courtyard of the tabernacle. For the south side, running its full length, make curtains of finely spun linen, a hundred cubits long on that side, **10** with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases, and silver hooks and bands on the posts. **11** Likewise, for the north side, make curtains a hundred cubits long, with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases, and silver hooks and bands on the posts. **12** For the width of the courtyard on the west side, make curtains fifty cubits wide, with ten posts and ten bases. **13** The width of the courtyard on the east side — the front — is to be fifty cubits. **14** On one side of the entrance, make curtains fifteen cubits wide, with three posts and three bases. **15** On the other side, make curtains fifteen cubits wide, with three posts and three bases. **16** For the entrance of the courtyard, make a screen twenty cubits wide, of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen — the work of an embroiderer — with four posts and four bases. **17** All the posts around the courtyard are to have silver bands, silver hooks, and bronze bases. **18** The courtyard is to be a hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide, with curtains of finely spun linen five cubits tall, set on bronze bases. **19** All the equipment used in the service of the tabernacle, including all its tent pegs and all the tent pegs for the courtyard, are to be made of bronze."

---

### The Perpetual Lamp — Light That Never Goes Out (vv. 20–21)

**20** "Command the Sons of Israel to bring you pure oil of pressed olives for the light, to keep a lamp burning continually. **21** In the tent of meeting, outside the veil that is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons are to keep it burning from evening to morning in the presence of YHWH. This is to be a lasting ordinance for all generations among the Sons of Israel."

---

## Meaning Notes

**vv. 1–8 — THE BRONZE ALTAR: THE PLACE OF ENCOUNTER THROUGH SACRIFICE (Heb. *ve'asita eth-hammizbeach atsei shittim chamesh ammot orekh vechamesh ammot rochav ravua yihyeh hammizbeach veshalosh ammot qomato*, וְעָשִׂ֤יתָ אֶת־הַמִּזְבֵּ֙חַ֙ עֲצֵ֣י שִׁטִּ֔ים חָמֵ֥שׁ אַמּ֛וֹת אֹ֖רֶךְ וְחָמֵ֣שׁ אַמּ֣וֹת רֹ֑חַב רָב֥וּעַ יִהְיֶ֖ה הַמִּזְבֵּ֑חַ וְשָׁלֹ֥שׁ אַמּ֖וֹת קֹמָתֽוֹ):** The *mizbeach* (מִזְבֵּחַ, "altar") is the first thing encountered in the approach to God's dwelling. Its name derives from *zavach* (זָבַח, "to slaughter, to sacrifice"), making it literally "the place of slaughter." This is not euphemistic language; the altar is named for what happens on it. Before anyone enters the holy place, before anyone approaches the veil, before anyone encounters the lampstand or the bread of the Presence, they encounter the altar — the place where life is offered, where blood is shed, where the cost of approaching a holy God is made visible.

The altar is perfectly square (*ravua*, רָבוּעַ, "foursquare") — five cubits by five cubits, roughly seven and a half feet on each side. The square shape signals completeness, stability, and cosmic order: the altar faces all four directions, open on every side, accessible from any approach. Its height of three cubits (about four and a half feet) means the officiant must reach up to place offerings on it — the gesture of sacrifice is an upward gesture, a lifting.

The *qarnot* (קַרְנֹת, "horns") at each corner are the most symbolically loaded feature. The word *qeren* (קֶרֶן) means "horn" — the horn of a powerful animal, a symbol of strength, authority, and protection. The horns are not decorative; they are structural projections that rise from the four corners, and in later texts, blood is applied specifically to the horns (Lev. 4:7, 18, 25, 30). The horns of the altar become places of refuge (1 Kings 1:50–51; 2:28) — a person who grasps the horns of the altar appeals to the ultimate sanctuary. The phrase *mimmennu qarnotav* (מִמֶּ֖נּוּ קַרְנֹתָ֑יו, "its horns are from it") insists that the horns are not attached appendages but grow from the altar itself — they are organic to its identity. The altar's power and its protection are inherent, not added.

The overlay is *nechoshet* (נְחֹשֶׁת, "bronze/copper"), not gold. The altar stands outside the dwelling, in the courtyard, and the material hierarchy continues: gold inside the most holy place, silver at the veil's bases, bronze in the courtyard. Bronze is the metal of durability and heat-resistance — practical for a structure that will sustain fire constantly. But it is also the metal of judgment in prophetic imagery (Ezek. 1:7; Dan. 10:6; Rev. 1:15). The altar is bronze because it is the place where judgment and mercy intersect: the judgment that sin demands and the mercy that substitutionary sacrifice provides.

The utensils listed in v. 3 — *sirotav* (סִירֹתָיו, "its pots/pails," for removing fatty ashes), *ya'av* (יָעָיו, "its shovels"), *mizraqotav* (מִזְרְקֹתָיו, "its basins," for catching and throwing blood), *mizlagotav* (מִזְלְגֹתָיו, "its forks," for handling meat), and *machtotav* (מַחְתֹּתָיו, "its firepans/censers") — describe the working tools of sacrifice. These are not ceremonial ornaments but functional instruments for handling blood, ash, fire, and meat. The altar is a working altar, and the tools acknowledge the physical, visceral nature of what happens there. The LXX renders these with corresponding Greek terms, preserving the practical character of the list.

Verse 8 closes with a remarkable instruction: *nevuv luchot ta'aseh oto ka'asher her'ah otekha bahar ken ya'asu* (נְב֥וּב לֻחֹ֖ת תַּעֲשֶׂ֣ה אֹת֑וֹ כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר הֶרְאָ֥ה אֹתְךָ֛ בָּהָ֖ר כֵּ֥ן יַעֲשֽׂוּ, "hollow, of boards, you shall make it — as you were shown on the mountain, so shall they make it"). The altar is *nevuv* (נְבוּב, "hollow") — not solid but an open shell, a casing of boards. The fire burns on something that is itself empty inside. The heaviest piece of theological furniture in the courtyard is, structurally, empty at its core. And once again, the governing refrain: build it as you were shown. The pattern comes from the mountain. The earthly altar replicates a heavenly design.

**vv. 9–19 — THE COURTYARD: THE BOUNDARY BETWEEN COMMON AND SACRED (Heb. *ve'asita eth chatsar hammishkan*, וְעָשִׂ֕יתָ אֵ֖ת חֲצַ֣ר הַמִּשְׁכָּ֑ן):** The *chatsar* (חָצֵר, "courtyard, enclosure") defines the outer boundary of sacred space. The word comes from a root suggesting an enclosed or fenced area — a space that is marked off, set apart, bounded. The courtyard is not the dwelling itself but the space around it, the zone between the profane world of the camp and the holy interior of the tabernacle. It is transitional space — you have left the camp but not yet entered the dwelling.

The dimensions are precise: one hundred cubits long (roughly 150 feet) and fifty cubits wide (about 75 feet), with a height of five cubits (approximately 7.5 feet). The proportions are a 2:1 rectangle, oriented east-west with the entrance on the east — the direction of sunrise, the direction from which God's glory will later come (Ezek. 43:1–4). The curtains are *shesh moshezar* (שֵׁשׁ מׇשְׁזָר, "finely twisted linen") — white linen, without the blue, purple, and scarlet of the inner curtains. The courtyard walls are plain, undyed, unadorned. The color and artistry increase as one moves inward.

The posts (*ammudehem*, עַמּוּדֵיהֶם) stand in bronze bases (*adnei nechoshet*, אַדְנֵי נְחֹשֶׁת) with silver *chashugim* (חֲשׁוּקֵיהֶם, "bands, fillets") and silver *vavim* (וָוֵיהֶם, "hooks"). The LXX renders *chashugim* as something fastened or banded around the post — silver bands that wrap the post like rings. The combination of bronze bases and silver bands places the courtyard between the all-bronze world of the outer camp and the silver-and-gold world of the dwelling interior. The courtyard is intermediate — neither fully common nor fully holy, a zone of preparation and approach.

The entrance (*sha'ar*, שַׁעַר, implied by the *masakh*, מָסָךְ, "screen") on the east side is twenty cubits wide (about 30 feet) — a generous opening, wider than the fifteen-cubit curtain sections flanking it on either side. The screen is made of the same colored materials as the dwelling's entrance screen in 26:36 — blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen — and is the work of a *roqem* (רֹקֵם, "embroiderer"). The entrance to the courtyard is the one point of color visible to the approaching Israelite: a burst of blue, purple, and scarlet against the plain white linen walls. The invitation into sacred space is visible, vivid, and specific — there is one way in, and it is marked by beauty.

Verse 19 extends the bronze requirement to *kol kelei hammishkan bekhol avodato vekhol yetedotav vekhol yetedot hechatsar nechoshet* (כֹּ֣ל כְּלֵ֧י הַמִּשְׁכָּ֛ן בְּכֹ֥ל עֲבֹדָת֖וֹ וְכׇל־יְתֵדֹתָ֗יו וְכׇל־יְתֵדֹ֛ת הֶחָצֵ֖ר נְחֹֽשֶׁת, "all the equipment of the tabernacle for all its service, and all its pegs, and all the pegs of the courtyard — bronze"). The *yetedot* (יְתֵדֹת, "pegs, stakes") are the tent pegs that anchor the curtains to the ground — the humblest, most overlooked components of the structure. Even these are specified: bronze. Nothing in the tabernacle is accidental, nothing beneath notice, nothing left to the builder's discretion. The pegs that no one sees, driven into the ground and buried in sand, are made of the same material as the altar. The lowest serves the same God as the highest.

**vv. 20–21 — THE PERPETUAL LAMP: OIL AND OBLIGATION (Heb. *ve'attah tetsavveh et-benei yisra'el veyiqchu elekha shemen zayith zakh kathith lamma'or leha'alot ner tamid*, וְאַתָּ֞ה תְּצַוֶּ֣ה ׀ אֶת־בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל וְיִקְח֨וּ אֵלֶ֜יךָ שֶׁ֣מֶן זַ֥יִת זָ֛ךְ כָּתִ֖ית לַמָּא֑וֹר לְהַעֲלֹ֥ת נֵ֖ר תָּמִֽיד):** The chapter's final two verses shift from architecture to liturgy, from building to burning. The command is addressed to Moses directly (*ve'attah*, וְאַתָּה, "and you yourself") and through him to the *benei yisra'el* (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, "Sons of Israel"). The entire community is responsible for providing the oil; this is not a priestly task but a communal one. The lamp that burns before YHWH is sustained by the whole people.

The oil must be *shemen zayith zakh kathith* (שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זָךְ כָּתִית, "pure olive oil, beaten/pressed") — not oil extracted by grinding in a mill (which would include sediment from crushed pits) but oil produced by beating or pressing the olives, yielding the clearest, purest first-press oil. The word *zakh* (זָךְ, "pure, clear, clean") describes oil that is transparent, without cloudiness or impurity. The word *kathith* (כָּתִית, "beaten, crushed") comes from *kathath* (כָּתַת, "to beat, to crush, to pound") — the olive must be crushed to yield its light. The image is potent: what gives light must first be broken.

The purpose is *leha'alot ner tamid* (לְהַעֲלֹת נֵר תָּמִיד, "to cause a lamp to go up continually"). The verb *ha'alot* (הַעֲלֹת, from *alah*, עָלָה, "to go up, to ascend") is striking — one does not "light" a lamp in Hebrew; one "causes it to ascend." The flame is an ascent, a rising. And *tamid* (תָּמִיד, "continually, perpetually, regularly") does not necessarily mean the lamp never goes out (v. 21 specifies "from evening to morning") but that the practice is unbroken — every evening, without exception, the lamp is kindled and burns through the night. The *ner tamid* becomes one of the most enduring symbols in Jewish practice, surviving the destruction of both temples and continuing in every synagogue to this day.

The location is *be'ohel mo'ed michuts lapparokhet asher al-ha'edut* (בְּאֹ֣הֶל מוֹעֵ֗ד מִח֛וּץ לַפָּרֹ֜כֶת אֲשֶׁ֣ר עַל־הָעֵדֻ֗ת, "in the tent of meeting, outside the veil that is before the testimony"). The lamp burns in the holy place, not in the most holy place — outside the veil, in the room with the table and the lampstand. The light burns where the priests can see it, where the bread is laid out, where the daily service takes place. The most holy place, behind the veil, is in darkness — or rather, in a darkness that needs no lamp because the presence of God is its own light. The lamp serves the space of human approach, not the space of divine dwelling.

Aaron and his sons (*ya'arokh oto Aharon uvanav*, יַעֲרֹ֣ךְ אֹת֞וֹ אַהֲרֹ֧ן וּבָנָ֛יו) are to arrange or tend the lamp — the verb *arakh* (עָרַךְ) means "to arrange, to set in order, to tend." The priestly role here is maintenance, not initiation: the community provides the oil, the priests tend the flame. The closing phrase — *chuqqat olam ledorotam me'et benei yisra'el* (חֻקַּ֤ת עוֹלָם֙ לְדֹ֣רֹתָ֔ם מֵאֵ֖ת בְּנֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵֽל, "a lasting ordinance for their generations, from the Sons of Israel") — elevates this practice beyond the wilderness generation to a perpetual obligation. The lamp is not temporary like the tabernacle's location; it is permanent like the relationship it signifies. The LXX renders *chuqqat olam* as *nomimon aionion* (νόμιμον αἰώνιον, "an eternal ordinance"), underscoring the perpetual nature of the command.

---

## Translator's Reflection

**The outer frame of God's house — where fire, fabric, and faithfulness define the approach to the divine:**

Exodus 27 moves outward. After the ark and the table and the lampstand (ch. 25), after the curtains and frames and veil (ch. 26), the instructions now turn to the courtyard structures — the bronze altar, the linen-walled enclosure, and the perpetual lamp. These are the elements that define the approach: what you encounter first, what surrounds you as you draw near, and what burns through the darkness while you sleep.

**1. The altar stands at the center of the courtyard, and it stands for everything.** The bronze altar is the largest piece of furniture in the entire tabernacle complex and the first thing an Israelite encounters after entering the courtyard. It is a massive, hollow, bronze-covered structure with horns rising from its corners, and it dominates the space. Its centrality is theological: before you see the lampstand, before you smell the incense, before you glimpse the veil, you must reckon with the altar. The approach to God runs through the place of sacrifice. This is not a peripheral or optional feature of the tabernacle; it is the gateway. The altar says what the rest of the structure assumes — that the distance between a holy God and an unholy people can be crossed, but not cheaply, not casually, not without blood. The altar is square, open on all sides, accessible from every direction, because the need for atonement is universal. And the altar is hollow — structurally empty at its center — because the point of sacrifice is not the altar itself but what happens upon it: the offering that ascends, the life that is given, the smoke that rises.

**2. The courtyard creates a threshold the eye cannot ignore.** The hundred-by-fifty-cubit enclosure of white linen, five cubits tall, does something deceptively simple: it draws a line. Inside the curtains is sacred space; outside is the camp. The boundary is fabric, not stone — you could cut through it with a knife, walk around it in minutes, see over it if you stood on a friend's shoulders. The barrier is not physical but covenantal. The linen walls say: this space is different. The rules here are different. The God who is everywhere has chosen to be specifically here, and you must enter through the gate, not through the wall. The single entrance on the east side — the burst of blue, purple, and scarlet against the plain white linen — is the only way in. The courtyard teaches a lesson Israel will hear again and again: sacred space has a door, and the door matters.

**3. The material hierarchy preaches without words.** Bronze bases in the courtyard. Silver hooks on the posts. Silver bases under the veil inside. Gold everywhere in the most holy place. The progression is not decorative but theological — the metals tell you where you stand in relation to the divine presence. The closer you come, the more precious the material, the more costly the approach. A worshiper walking from the camp to the courtyard to the holy place to the most holy place would cross from earth to bronze to silver to gold — from the common ground of human existence to the radiant core of divine holiness. The tabernacle does not merely house God's presence; it maps the journey toward it, and it marks every step of that journey with increasing costliness. The architecture says: drawing near to God is possible, but it is not trivial. Each threshold costs more than the last.

**4. The tent pegs are bronze.** Verse 19 is easy to overlook: all the tent pegs — for the tabernacle and for the courtyard — are to be made of bronze. Tent pegs are driven into the ground and forgotten. They hold nothing up; they hold everything down. They are invisible to anyone inside the structure. And yet God specifies their material. The pegs that anchor the tabernacle to the desert floor are made of the same metal as the altar where sacrifices burn. Nothing in the sacred structure is beneath divine attention. The pegs in the sand and the horns on the altar share the same specification, the same care, the same material. If God notices the tent pegs, then every component of faithfulness matters — not just the visible acts of worship but the hidden anchors that keep the whole structure from blowing away in the wind.

**5. The lamp burns through the night because someone crushed the olives.** The chapter's closing command shifts from construction to cultivation, from what is built to what is sustained. The perpetual lamp requires two things: oil from the community and tending from the priests. The oil must be *zakh kathith* — pure and beaten, the finest first-press olive oil, produced by crushing. The image is unforgettable: the light that burns before God comes from something broken. The olive is crushed, and out of the crushing comes clear oil that feeds a flame that burns all night in a room where no one sleeps. The lamp serves no practical function — the priests are not reading by it, not cooking by it, not navigating by it. It burns as testimony: someone is here. Someone is awake. The presence has not departed. The flame that rises in the holy place is a response to a command that spans all generations — *chuqqat olam* — and every night it burns, it says to the darkness what Israel has been saying since Sinai: YHWH is in this place.

**6. From evening to morning — the God of the night watch.** The lamp burns *me'erev ad-boqer* (מֵעֶ֣רֶב עַד־בֹּ֔קֶר, "from evening to morning"). Not during the day, when the sun provides light, but through the night, when the camp sleeps and the wilderness is dark and the fears are greatest. The God of the tabernacle keeps a night light burning. The lamp does not illuminate the darkness for God — he does not need it. It illuminates the darkness for Israel. It says: while you sleep, the flame still burns. While the camp is vulnerable, the presence has not withdrawn. The tabernacle's most enduring image is not the gold of the ark or the blood of the altar but a small flame burning through the desert night, tended by priests, fueled by crushed olives, witnessed only by the veil — and by the God on the other side of it.

The outer frame of God's house is defined by fire, fabric, and faithfulness. The altar burns with the fire of sacrifice. The courtyard hangs with the fabric of separation. The lamp burns with the faithfulness of a God who does not sleep. And together, they form the approach — the path from the ordinary world into the presence of the Holy One, one step at a time, one threshold at a time, one night at a time, until morning comes.

— *PNEUMA*
